---
title: "Biology R assignment"
author: "Ofure Okogun"
date: "2026-01-19"
output: html_document
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

## R Markdown

This is an R Markdown document. Markdown is a simple formatting syntax for authoring HTML, PDF, and MS Word documents. For more details on using R Markdown see <http://rmarkdown.rstudio.com>.

When you click the **Knit** button a document will be generated that includes both content as well as the output of any embedded R code chunks within the document. You can embed an R code chunk like this:

```{r cars}
summary(cars)
```

## Including Plots

You can also embed plots, for example:

```{r pressure, echo=FALSE}
plot(pressure)
```

Note that the `echo = FALSE` parameter was added to the code chunk to prevent printing of the R code that generated the plot.
``` {r bio}
install.packages("dplyr")
library(dplyr)
MooseDataset<- na.omit(MooseDataset)
MooseDataset<- select(MooseDataset, Ecoregion, Year, Area, Estimated_Moose_Pop)
min(MooseDataset$Year)
#the oldest year is 1904
max(MooseDataset$Estimated_Moose_Pop)
#the highest Estimated_Moose_Pop is 41250
MooseDataset <- mutate(MooseDataset, MooseDensity = Estimated_Moose_Pop / Area)
plot(MooseDataset$Year, MooseDataset$MooseDensity, 
     xlab = "year", 
     ylab = "Moose per sq km", 
     main = "Moose density in Newfoundland ecoregions over time")
MooseDataWest <- filter(MooseDataset, Ecoregion == "Western_Forests")
plot(MooseDataWest$Year, MooseDataWest$MooseDensity, 
        xlab = "year", 
        ylab = "Moose per sq km",
        type = "l",
        col= "maroon",
        main = "Moose density in Western Forest region over time")
Moosedatayear<- filter(MooseDataset, Year == "2020")
Moosedensity2020<- filter(Moosedatayear, MooseDensity > 2.0)
arrange(Moosedensity2020, desc(MooseDensity))
MooseData_final <- MooseDataset %>%
  filter(Year == 2020) %>%
  filter(MooseDensity > 2.0) %>%
  arrange(desc(MooseDensity)) %>%
  print()
  Saplings<- na.omit(SaplingStudy)
sap_reg_browse<- Saplings %>%
 group_by(Ecoregion) %>%
 summarize(mean_moose_BrowsingScore=mean(BrowsingScore, na.rm= TRUE))
print(sap_reg_browse)
avg_browse_reg<- sap_reg_browse 
arrange(avg_browse_reg, desc(mean_moose_BrowsingScore)) 
sap_reg_height<- Saplings %>%
group_by(Ecoregion) %>%
summarize(mean_moose_Height=mean(Height, na.rm= TRUE)) %>%
print()
sap_reg_height_low<- sap_reg_height %>%
  filter(mean_moose_Height< 20) %>%
  arrange(desc(mean_moose_Height)) %>%
  print()
#Northern_Peninsula_Forests and Western_Forests have heights less that 20cm
sap_spe_browse<- Saplings %>%
  group_by(Species) %>%
  summarise(BrowsingScore=mean(BrowsingScore, na.rm= TRUE)) %>%
  print()
avg_browse_spe<- sap_spe_browse %>%
  arrange(desc(BrowsingScore)) %>%
  print()
#Black_Ash has the highest browsing score, and Black__Spruce has the lowest browsing score
fir_reg_browse<- Saplings %>%
  filter(Species=="Balsam_Fir") %>%
  group_by(Ecoregion) %>%
  summarise(mean_moose_BrowsingScore=mean(BrowsingScore, na.rm= TRUE)) %>%
  print()
barplot(fir_reg_browse$mean_moose_BrowsingScore, names.arg = fir_reg_browse$Ecoregion, xlab = "Ecoregion", ylab = "Mean Moose Browsing Score", main = "Balsam Fir Browsing", col = "plum", cex.names = 0.5, las = 2)
spruce_reg_browse<- Saplings %>%
  filter(Species=="Black_Spruce") %>%
  group_by(Ecoregion) %>%
  summarise(mean_moose_BrowsingScore=mean(BrowsingScore,na.rm= TRUE)) %>%
  print()
barplot(spruce_reg_browse$mean_moose_BrowsingScore, names.arg = spruce_reg_browse$Ecoregion, xlab = "Ecoregion", ylab = "Mean Moose Browsing Score", main = "Black Spruce Browsing", col = "salmon", cex.names = 0.5, las = 2)
#Black spruce has no browsing in Maritime_Barrens and EasternHyperOceanicBarrens
#The highest browsing region for both is Nothern_Peninsula_Forests
sap_reg_tally<- Saplings %>%
  group_by(Ecoregion) %>%
  tally() %>% 
  print()
sap_spe_tally<- Saplings %>%
  group_by(Species) %>%
  tally() %>% 
  print()
#I believe that Balsam firs are overrepresented, while Black ash is underrepresented. I also believe StraitOfBelleIsleBarrens could be represented more.
#Recognizing biases ensures the validity of research findings. It also confirms the effectiveness of conservation strategies.
```
